lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Give him this money and these notes, Reynaldo.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: I will, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You shall do marvellous wisely, good Reynaldo,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Before you visit him, to make inquire

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Of his behavior.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: My lord, I did intend it.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Marry, well said; very well said. Look you, sir,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Inquire me first what Danskers are in Paris;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And how, and who, what means, and where they keep,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What company, at what expense; and finding

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: By this encompassment and drift of question

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That they do know my son, come you more nearer

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Than your particular demands will touch it:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Take you, as ’twere, some distant knowledge of him;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As thus, “I know his father and his friends,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And in part him:” do you mark this, Reynaldo?

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: Ay, very well, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: “And in part him; but” you may say “not well:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But, if’t be he I mean, he’s very wild;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Addicted so-and-so:” and there put on him

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What forgeries you please; marry, none so rank

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As may dishonour him; take heed of that;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: But, sir, such wanton, wild and usual slips

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As are companions noted and most known

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: To youth and liberty.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: As gaming, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Ay, or drinking, fencing, swearing, quarrelling,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Drabbing: you may go so far.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: My lord, that would dishonour him.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: ’Faith, no; as you may season it in the charge.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You must not put another scandal on him,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That he is open to incontinency;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That’s not my meaning: but breathe his faults so quaintly

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That they may seem the taints of liberty,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The flash and outbreak of a fiery mind,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: A savageness in unreclaimed blood,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Of general assault.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: But, my good lord—

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Wherefore should you do this?

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: Ay, my lord,

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: I would know that.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Marry, sir, here’s my drift;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And, I believe, it is a fetch of wit:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: You laying these slight sullies on my son,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As ’twere a thing a little soil’d i’ the working,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Mark you,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Your party in converse, him you would sound,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Having ever seen in the prenominate crimes

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The youth you breathe of guilty, be assured

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: He closes with you in this consequence;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: “Good sir,” or so, or “friend,” or “gentleman,”

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: According to the phrase or the addition

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Of man and country.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: Very good, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And then, sir, does he this—he does—what was I about to say?

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: By the mass, I was about to say something: where did I

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: leave?

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: At “closes in the consequence,” at “friend or so,” and “gentleman.”

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: At “closes in the consequence,” ay, marry;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: He closes thus: “I know the gentleman;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I saw him yesterday, or t’ other day,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Or then, or then; with such, or such; and, as you say,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: There was a’ gaming; there o’ertook in’s rouse;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: There falling out at tennis:” or perchance,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: “I saw him enter such a house of sale,”

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Videlicet, a brothel, or so forth.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: See you now;

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Your bait of falsehood takes this carp of truth:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And thus do we of wisdom and of reach,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: With windlasses and with assays of bias,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: By indirections find directions out:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: So by my former lecture and advice,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Shall you my son. You have me, have you not?

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: My lord, I have.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: God be wi’ you; fare you well.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: Good my lord!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Observe his inclination in yourself.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: I shall, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And let him ply his music.

lines.speaker: REYNALDO
lines.text: Well, my lord.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Farewell!

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: O, my lord, my lord, I have been so affrighted!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: With what, i’ the name of God?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: My lord, as I was sewing in my closet,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Lord Hamlet, with his doublet all unbraced;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: No hat upon his head; his stockings foul’d,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Ungarter’d, and down-gyved to his ankle;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Pale as his shirt; his knees knocking each other;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And with a look so piteous in purport

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: As if he had been loosed out of hell

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: To speak of horrors—he comes before me.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Mad for thy love?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: My lord, I do not know;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: But truly, I do fear it.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What said he?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: He took me by the wrist and held me hard;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: Then goes he to the length of all his arm;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And, with his other hand thus o’er his brow,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: He falls to such perusal of my face

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: As he would draw it. Long stay’d he so;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: At last, a little shaking of mine arm

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And thrice his head thus waving up and down,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: He raised a sigh so piteous and profound

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: As it did seem to shatter all his bulk

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And end his being: that done, he lets me go:

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And, with his head over his shoulder turn’d,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: He seem’d to find his way without his eyes;

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: For out o’ doors he went without their helps,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: And, to the last, bended their light on me.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Come, go with me: I will go seek the king.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: This is the very ecstasy of love,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Whose violent property fordoes itself

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And leads the will to desperate undertakings

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As oft as any passion under heaven

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That does afflict our natures. I am sorry.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: What, have you given him any hard words of late?

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: No, my good lord, but, as you did command,

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: I did repel his letters and denied

lines.speaker: OPHELIA
lines.text: His access to me.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: That hath made him mad.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I am sorry that with better heed and judgment

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I had not quoted him: I fear’d he did but trifle,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And meant to wreck thee; but, beshrew my jealousy!

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: By heaven, it is as proper to our age

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: To cast beyond ourselves in our opinions

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: As it is common for the younger sort

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: To lack discretion. Come, go we to the king:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: This must be known; which, being kept close, might move

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: More grief to hide than hate to utter love.

stage_directions.text: How now, Ophelia! what’s the matter?

glosses.phrase: flash and outbreak of a fiery mind
closes with you in this consequence
glosses.definition: agrees with you along these lines

glosses.phrase: windlasses and with assays of bias
leads the will to desperate undertakings
glosses.definition: drives the mind to rash acts

annotations.title: Polonius's indirections and the poetics of surveillance
annotations.body: Polonius instructs Reynaldo to investigate Laertes by a technique he calls 'indirections find directions out': gossip about the son's drabbing, drinking, and quarreling to elicit, from the listener, confirmation of the son's true behavior. The method is a miniature of Polonius's whole epistemology, and by extension of Claudius's court. No one asks direct questions because no one can afford honest answers. The scene is often cut in performance but is theoretically crucial: it establishes, before we see Hamlet's 'antic disposition' exposed, that this is a state in which surveillance has replaced trust. When Polonius later plans to 'loose' Ophelia to Hamlet and eavesdrop, the technique will be the same.
sources: John Dover Wilson, What Happens in Hamlet (1935)

annotations.title: Ophelia's report: a scene Shakespeare does not stage
annotations.body: The encounter between Hamlet and Ophelia that Ophelia describes — Hamlet silent, ungartered, pale, holding her wrist and studying her face — is never shown on stage. Shakespeare consistently reports through speech the most intimate crises in the play (Ophelia's drowning, the pirate attack, the England voyage), staging instead the public consequences. The strategy concentrates dramatic power: the unseen scene becomes more vivid in Ophelia's telling than it could in a staged rendering. Editors from Dover Wilson to Harold Jenkins have debated whether Hamlet's visit signals genuine distress or the beginning of the 'antic disposition' — a question the play refuses to settle because Ophelia herself cannot tell.

annotations.title: The father's error — 'I am sorry'
annotations.body: Polonius's response to Ophelia's report is revealing: 'This is the very ecstasy of love,' and he assumes her obedient refusal to admit Hamlet's letters has caused the madness. The comic irony is that Polonius is half-right and wholly wrong: Hamlet's distress is real but not about love, and Polonius's remedy — to march Ophelia to the King and weaponize her distress for political intelligence — will catalyze the next four acts of catastrophe. The line 'I had not quoted him' ('I had not interpreted him') is the scene's quiet admission that Polonius has misread everything, and he will now commit Ophelia, Hamlet, and himself to acting on that misreading.
sources: Carol Thomas Neely, Broken Nuptials in Shakespeare's Plays (1985)

trials.prompt: What does Polonius instruct Reynaldo to do in Paris?
options: Deliver a sealed letter directly to Laertes.
Spread rumors about Laertes's misbehavior to see who confirms them.
Confront Laertes about his debts and drinking.
Arrange for Laertes to return home at once.

trials.prompt: What does Polonius mean by 'indirections find directions out'?
options: Take the long road so you are not followed.
Roundabout methods uncover straight truths.
Lies always reveal themselves in time.
Young men go astray before they find their path.

trials.prompt: How does Ophelia describe Hamlet's appearance when he visited her closet?
options: Elegantly dressed and composed, reciting a love poem.
Disheveled — doublet unbraced, stockings fouled and fallen, pale as his shirt, staring at her silently.
Armed and furious, accusing her of betrayal.
Laughing wildly and throwing letters into the fire.

trials.prompt: What does Polonius conclude is the cause of Hamlet's distress?
options: Political anxiety about the Norwegian threat.
The 'very ecstasy of love' — Ophelia's refusal to see him has driven him mad.
Grief for his father that has festered into melancholy.
Fear of being deposed by Claudius.

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic function of this scene?
options: To establish Reynaldo as a major character in later acts.
To introduce a new subplot about Laertes's education.
To set up Polonius's erroneous diagnosis, which will drive the eavesdropping of Act 3.
To provide comic relief before the play's first soliloquy.
